title: Generafi Payroll Rules Engine
subtitle: Rebuilding Moroccan payroll and accounting around versioned compliance rules
description: A technical case study on turning fast-changing Moroccan payroll and accounting requirements into auditable, tenant-safe product infrastructure.

images.alt: Generafi dashboard showing payroll and accounting operations
images.caption: Primary workflow surface for payroll, accounting, and compliance activity.

images.alt: Detailed Generafi dashboard controls for finance operators
images.caption: Operational controls are organized around review, approval, and audit readiness.

sections.title: Why the product needed a rebuild
paragraphs: Generafi started from a practical problem: Moroccan SMBs need accounting and payroll software that understands local compliance instead of forcing teams to translate generic workflows into Moroccan operating reality.

The original architecture could support basic workflows, but it made every new compliance change expensive. Payroll rules, permissions, and audit trails were too tightly coupled to application logic, so the rebuild focused on making compliance an explicit part of the data model.

pullQuote.text: The goal was not to make payroll configurable. The goal was to make every calculation explainable later.

sections.title: The questions we had to answer

decisions.title: How should payroll rules change without rewriting application code?
decisions.teaser: Regulatory changes are normal, so the system needed a durable way to model them.
decisions.answer: Payroll rules became first-class records with schemas, effective dates, versions, and test fixtures. A payroll run locks to the applicable rule version, which means historical calculations remain reproducible even after new rules are published.
alternatives: Hardcode calculations directly in service logic
Keep rules in spreadsheets and import results
Use a separate rules service before the domain model was stable

decisions.title: How do we isolate tenants without making operations expensive?
decisions.teaser: The product needs shared infrastructure economics without casual data leakage risk.
decisions.answer: The system uses tenant-scoped data access and audit checks at mutation boundaries. Every business object carries tenant ownership, and service methods require tenant context before reads or writes can happen.
alternatives: Application-only filtering with no database guardrails
A hybrid model that separates only payroll records

decisions.title: What should permissions model for Moroccan SMBs look like?
decisions.teaser: Real organizations do not always match fixed admin, manager, and employee hierarchies.
decisions.answer: The permissions model is action-based rather than title-based. Customers can define roles that match their accounting, payroll, and approval workflows while the product keeps high-risk operations behind explicit capabilities.
alternatives: Full attribute-based access control from day one
One owner account with lightweight collaborator access

callout.text: The most important architecture boundary is between rules that can change and records that must never change retroactively.

designDecision.title: Versioned Rules as Domain Data
designDecision.description: Tax, payroll, and deduction rules are represented as versioned domain records with validation and effective dates.
designDecision.why: This keeps compliance updates operationally manageable while preserving a clear audit path for past payroll runs.

constraints.title: Compliance changes are recurring
constraints.description: The system has to assume tax and payroll rules will evolve several times a year.

constraints.title: Historical payroll must be reproducible
constraints.description: A past payroll run cannot silently change because a rule was edited later.

constraints.title: Operators need confidence, not just automation
constraints.description: Finance teams need to see why a calculation happened before they trust the system.

sections.title: What we chose not to do

tradeoffs.title: No marketplace of integrations at launch
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Build a small set of deliberate import and export paths around core payroll and accounting workflows.
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Customers with unusual toolchains need custom handling during onboarding.
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When repeated integration requests show stable demand patterns.

tradeoffs.title: No full no-code rule builder
tradeoffs.whatWeChose: Expose rules as structured configuration managed by trusted operators.
tradeoffs.whatItCost: Non-technical users cannot create arbitrary payroll logic themselves.
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: When rule schemas stabilize enough to make a constrained builder safe.

milestones.title: Domain mapping
milestones.description: Mapped payroll, accounting, and compliance workflows into entities that could be audited independently.

milestones.title: Tenant and permission foundation
milestones.description: Built tenant-scoped access patterns, role capabilities, and mutation audit events.

milestones.title: Rules engine prototype
milestones.description: Modeled versioned payroll rules and tested historical calculation replay.

milestones.title: Operator workflow refinement
milestones.description: Shifted interface work toward review, approval, and exception handling.

metrics.label: Rule families
metrics.description: Core payroll and deduction families represented in the rules model.

metrics.label: Audit coverage
metrics.description: Target coverage for domain mutations that affect payroll or accounting records.

metrics.label: Replay checks
metrics.description: Historical-style scenarios used to verify versioned rule behavior.

metrics.label: Tenant scope
metrics.description: Every domain operation requires explicit tenant context.

reflections.insight: Compliance-heavy products need a data model that explains decisions after the fact, not just a UI that helps users complete tasks.
reflections.transferable: Treat regulatory logic as product infrastructure early, especially when customers need auditability.

reflections.insight: Versioning only the rule is not enough. The inputs, dependencies, and approval state around a calculation need to be preserved too.
reflections.transferable: Design replayability around the whole calculation context.

sections.title: The work ahead
sections.intro: The next phase is about turning the architecture into a reliable operator experience.

items.title: Exception workflows
items.description: Make unusual payroll cases visible, reviewable, and easy to resolve without breaking auditability.

items.title: Reporting layer
items.description: Expose payroll and accounting history through reports that finance teams can trust.

items.title: Integration boundaries
items.description: Define stable import and export contracts before expanding partner integrations.

problem: Moroccan payroll rules change often, but historical payroll calculations need to remain reproducible and auditable.
solution: Model payroll rules as versioned domain objects with tenant-scoped execution, audit events, and replayable calculation context.
role: Domain modeling, payroll rules architecture, and audit workflow design.
proof: The case study defines rule families, tenant boundaries, replay fixtures, and audit coverage targets for compliance-heavy payroll work.
impact: A versioned rules model makes compliance changes safer while giving operators and auditors a clear record of how each payroll result was produced.
challenges: Keeping historical calculations stable after rule changes
Scoping every payroll operation to the correct tenant
Making compliance behavior explainable to operators and auditors
learnings: Compliance-heavy products need a data model that can explain decisions after the fact.
Replayability depends on preserving inputs, dependencies, and approval state, not only the rule version.
